blocks:
Ayahs

وَٱلْعَصْرِ (١)
By Time! (1)
إِنَّ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنَ لَفِى خُسْرٍ (٢)
Man is indeed in loss, (2)
إِلَّا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ وَتَوَاصَوْا۟ بِٱلْحَقِّ وَتَوَاصَوْا۟ بِٱلصَّبْرِ (٣)
except those who have faith and do righteous deeds, and enjoin one another to [follow] the truth, and enjoin one another to patience. (3)

(Commentary)

The Surah summarizes all the Quranic sciences (المعارف القرآنية) and gathers the scattered objectives of the Quran in the briefest of statements. It could potentially be either Meccan or Medinan, though it bears a closer resemblance to the Meccan surahs.

His saying, the Exalted: وَالْعَصْرِ — By the afternoon! This is an oath by time (العصر). Given that the two subsequent verses encompass the inclusion of all humanity in loss (الخسران) except those who follow the truth and are patient upon it—namely the believers who perform righteous deeds—it is most appropriate that "the time" (العصر) refers to the era of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله. This was the era of the rising of Islam upon human society and the manifestation of truth over falsehood.

It has also been said that it refers to the time of the afternoon (وقت العصر), which is the final part of the day, because of its indication of Divine governance (التدبير الربوبي) through the retreating of the day, the approaching of the night, and the departure of the sun's authority. Others have said it refers to the afternoon prayer (صلاة العصر), which is the "middle prayer" (الصلاة الوسطى) and the most virtuous of the daily obligatory prayers. It has also been said to mean the night and the day, as they are both called "the two times" (العصران). Others suggest it refers to time (الدهر) itself, due to the wondrous events it contains that point toward Divine power (القدرة الربوبية), among other interpretations.

It has been mentioned in some narrations that it refers to the era (عصر) of the appearance of al-Mahdi عليه‌السلام, because of the complete manifestation of truth over falsehood therein.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ الْإِنْسانَ لَفِي خُسْرٍ — Indeed man is in loss, The word "man" (الإنسان) here refers to the human genus. Loss (الخسر), ruin (الخسران), and detriment (الخسارة) signify the diminution of capital (رأس المال). Al-Raghib said: "This is attributed to the person, so it is said: 'So-and-so lost' (خسر فلان); and to the action, so it is said: 'His trade was lost' (خسرت تجارته)." The indefinite form (التنكير) of "loss" (خسر) is for magnification (التعظيم), or it may imply categorization (التنويع)—meaning a type of loss other than financial or social loss. The Exalted says: الَّذِينَ خَسِرُوا أَنْفُسَهُمْ وَأَهْلِيهِمْ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ أَلا ذلِكَ هُوَ الْخُسْرانُ الْمُبِينُ — those who have ruined themselves and their families on the Day of Resurrection. Look! That is indeed a manifest loss. (39:15).

His saying, the Exalted: إِلَّا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ — except those who have faith and do righteous deeds, This is an exception from the genus of man who is in loss. Those excepted are the individuals characterized by faith (الإيمان) and righteous deeds (الأعمال الصالحة); they are secure from loss.

This is because the Book of Allah clarifies that man possesses an eternal, everlasting life that is not severed by death. Rather, death is merely a transition from one abode to another, as previously discussed in the commentary on His saying, the Exalted: عَلى أَنْ نُبَدِّلَ أَمْثالَكُمْ وَنُنْشِئَكُمْ فِي ما لا تَعْلَمُونَ — from replacing you with your likes and recreating you in [a condition] you do not know. (56:61). It also clarifies that a portion of this life—the worldly life (الحياة الدنيا)—is a life of trial (امتحانية) by which the quality of the final portion is determined, which is the eternal afterlife (الحياة الآخرة), whether in felicity or wretchedness. The Exalted says: وَمَا الْحَياةُ الدُّنْيا فِي الْآخِرَةِ إِلَّا مَتاعٌ — The life of this world is nothing but a [passing] enjoyment compared to the Hereafter. (13:26), and He says: كُلُّ نَفْسٍ ذائِقَةُ الْمَوْتِ وَنَبْلُوكُمْ بِالشَّرِّ وَالْخَيْرِ فِتْنَةً — Every soul shall taste death, and We will test you with good and ill by way of trial. (21:35).

It further clarifies that the role of this life as a precursor to that life is through its manifestations of belief (الاعتقاد) and action (العمل). Thus, true belief (الاعتقاد الحق) and righteous deeds (العمل الصالح) are the criteria for otherworldly felicity, while disbelief (الكفر) and transgression (الفسوق) are the criteria for wretchedness therein. The Exalted says: وَأَنْ لَيْسَ لِلْإِنْسانِ إِلَّا ما سَعى وَأَنَّ سَعْيَهُ سوف يرى ثُمَّ يُجْزاهُ الْجَزاءَ الْأَوْفى — and that nothing belongs to man except what he strives for, and that he will soon be shown his endeavour, then he will be rewarded for it with the fullest reward, (53:39-41), and He says: مَنْ كَفَرَ فَعَلَيْهِ كُفْرُهُ وَمَنْ عَمِلَ صالِحاً فَلِأَنْفُسِهِمْ يَمْهَدُونَ — Whoever disbelieves, his disbelief shall be to his own detriment, and those who act righteously, they make provision for themselves, (30:44), and He says: مَنْ عَمِلَ صالِحاً فَلِنَفْسِهِ وَمَنْ أَساءَ فَعَلَيْها — Whoever acts righteously, it is for his own soul, and whoever does evil, it is to its detriment. (41:46). Allah has named what man will encounter in the Hereafter as "recompense" (جزاء) and "reward" (أجر) in many verses.

From all this, it becomes clear that life is the capital (رأس مال) for man, by which he earns that which he will live upon in his afterlife. If he follows the truth in conviction and action, his trade has profited, his earnings are blessed, and he is secure from evil in his future. But if he follows falsehood and turns away from faith and righteous deeds, his trade has lost, and he is deprived of good in his end. This is the meaning of His saying: إِنَّ الْإِنْسانَ لَفِي خُسْرٍ إِلَّا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ — Indeed man is in loss, except those who have faith and do righteous deeds.

What is meant by faith (الإيمان) is faith in Allah, and included in faith in Allah is faith in all His messengers and faith in the Last Day. The Exalted has explicitly stated that whoever does not believe in some of His messengers (1) or in the Last Day is not a believer in Allah.

The apparent meaning of His saying: وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ — and do righteous deeds—is being characterized by all righteous deeds. Thus, the exception does not include the transgressors (الفساق) among the believers who abandon some righteous acts. The implication is that loss (الخسر) is broader than the loss in all aspects of one's life—as is the case with the disbeliever who stubbornly opposes the truth and is immortalized in punishment—and loss in some aspects of one's life, such as the transgressing believer who is not immortalized in the Fire and whose punishment is eventually severed through intercession (الشفاعة) — the act of mediating on behalf of others before Allah — or similar means.

His saying, the Exalted: وَتَواصَوْا بِالْحَقِّ وَتَواصَوْا بِالصَّبْرِ — and enjoin one another to [follow] the truth, and enjoin one another to patience. Enjoining one another to the truth (التواصي بالحق) means that they advise one another regarding the truth—that is, to follow it and remain steadfast upon it. For the true religion (دين الحق) is nothing but following the truth in belief and action. Enjoining the truth is broader than "enjoining the right and forbidding the wrong" (الأمر بالمعروف والنهي عن المنكر), as it encompasses matters of belief (الاعتقاديات) and absolute encouragement and urging toward righteous deeds.

Furthermore, enjoining the truth is itself a righteous deed; mentioning it after "righteous deeds" is an instance of mentioning the specific (الخاص) after the general (العام) to emphasize its importance. Similarly, enjoining patience (التواصي بالصبر) is a part of enjoining the truth, and mentioning it afterward is another instance of mentioning the specific after the general to emphasize its importance. This is reinforced by the repetition of the word "enjoin" (تواصوا) where He said: وَتَواصَوْا بِالصَّبْرِ — and enjoin one another to patience—rather than saying "and enjoin the truth and patience."

In summary, the mention of their enjoining one another to the truth and to patience after mentioning their characterization by faith and righteous deeds points to the life of their hearts and the expansion of their breasts (انشراح صدورهم) for submission (الإسلام) to Allah. They possess a special concern and complete care for the manifestation of the authority of truth and its expansion among the people so that it may be followed and its following may endure. The Exalted says: أَفَمَنْ شَرَحَ اللهُ صَدْرَهُ لِلْإِسْلامِ فَهُوَ عَلى نُورٍ مِنْ رَبِّهِ فَوَيْلٌ لِلْقاسِيَةِ قُلُوبُهُمْ مِنْ ذِكْرِ اللهِ أُولئِكَ فِي ضَلالٍ مُبِينٍ — Is he whose breast Allah has opened to Islam so that he follows a light from his Lord [like those who are hard-hearted]? So woe to those whose hearts are hardened against the remembrance of Allah! They are in manifest error. (39:22).

Patience (الصبر) has been mentioned in an absolute sense, so it refers to something broader than patience in obeying Allah, patience in refraining from His disobedience, and patience during the calamities that strike one by the decree (قضاء) and measure (قدر) of Allah.

(1) An-Nisa: 150-151.

(Discussion Regarding Narrations)

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, with its chain of narration from Abd al-Rahman ibn Kathir, from Abu Abdillah عليه‌السلام regarding His saying, the Exalted: إِلَّا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا — except those who have faith—etc., he said: "He excepted the elite of His creation."

I say: In the remainder of the narration, he applied "faith" to faith in the guardianship (الولاية) of Ali عليه‌السلام, and "enjoining the truth" to their enjoining their descendants and successors with it.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Ibn Abbas regarding His saying: وَالْعَصْرِ إِنَّ الْإِنْسانَ لَفِي خُسْرٍ — By the afternoon! Indeed man is in loss—meaning Abu Jahl ibn Hisham; إِلَّا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ — except those who have faith and do righteous deeds—he mentioned Ali and Salman.
